is a [rail vehicle](/wiki/Rail_transport "Rail transport") that provides the motive power for a [train](/wiki/Train "Train"). Traditionally, locomotives pulled trains from the front. However, [push–pull](/wiki/Push%E2%80%93pull_train "Push–pull train") operation has become common, and in the pursuit for longer and heavier freight trains, companies are increasingly using [distributed power](/wiki/Distributed_power "Distributed power"): single or multiple locomotives placed at the front and rear and at intermediate points throughout the train under the control of the leading locomotive.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

A **locomotive** is a [rail vehicle](/wiki/Rail_transport "Rail transport") that provides the motive power for a [train](/wiki/Train "Train"). Traditionally, locomotives pulled trains from the front. However, [push–pull](/wiki/Push%E2%80%93pull_train "Push–pull train") operation has become common, and in the pursuit for longer and heavier freight trains, companies are increasingly using [distributed power](/wiki/Distributed_power "Distributed power"): single or multiple locomotives placed at the front and rear and at intermediate points throughout the train under the control of the leading locomotive.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

## Etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Locomotive&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Etymology")]

The word *locomotive* originates from the [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language") *loco* 'from a place', [ablative](/wiki/Ablative_case "Ablative case") of *locus* 'place', and the [Medieval Latin](/wiki/Medieval_Latin "Medieval Latin") *motivus* 'causing motion', and is a shortened form of the term *locomotive engine*,[[2]](#cite_note-2) which was first used in 1814[[3]](#cite_note-3) to distinguish between self-propelled and [stationary steam engines](/wiki/Stationary_steam_engine "Stationary steam engine").

## Classifications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Locomotive&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Classifications")]

See also: [Class (locomotive)](/wiki/Class_(locomotive) "Class (locomotive)")

Prior to locomotives, the motive force for railways had been generated by various lower-technology methods such as human power, horse power, [gravity](/wiki/Gravity_railroad "Gravity railroad") or stationary engines that drove cable systems. Few such systems are still in existence today. Locomotives may generate their power from fuel (wood, coal, petroleum or natural gas), or they may take [power](/wiki/Power_(physics) "Power (physics)") from an outside source of electricity. It is common to classify locomotives by their source of energy. The common ones include:

### Steam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Locomotive&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Steam")]

Main article: [Steam locomotive](/wiki/Steam_locomotive "Steam locomotive")

A steam locomotive is a locomotive whose primary power source is a [steam engine](/wiki/Steam_engine "Steam engine"). The most common form of steam locomotive also contains a [boiler](/wiki/Boiler_(power_generation) "Boiler (power generation)") to generate the steam used by the engine. The water in the boiler is heated by burning combustible material – usually coal, wood, or oil – to produce steam. The steam moves reciprocating [pistons](/wiki/Piston "Piston") which are connected to the locomotive's main wheels, known as the "[driving wheels](/wiki/Driving_wheel "Driving wheel")". Both fuel and water supplies are carried with the locomotive, either on the locomotive itself, in [bunkers](/wiki/Fuel_bunker "Fuel bunker") and [tanks](/wiki/Water_tank "Water tank"), (this arrangement is known as a "[tank locomotive](/wiki/Tank_locomotive "Tank locomotive")") or pulled behind the locomotive, in [tenders](/wiki/Tender_(rail) "Tender (rail)"), (this arrangement is known as a "[tender locomotive](/wiki/Tender_(rail) "Tender (rail)")").

The first full-scale working railway steam locomotive was built by [Richard Trevithick](/wiki/Richard_Trevithick "Richard Trevithick") in 1802. It was constructed for the [Coalbrookdale](/wiki/Coalbrookdale "Coalbrookdale") ironworks in [Shropshire](/wiki/Shropshire "Shropshire") in England though no record of it working there has survived.[[4]](#cite_note-4) On 21 February 1804, the first recorded steam-hauled railway journey took place as another of Trevithick's locomotives hauled a train from the [Penydarren](/wiki/Penydarren "Penydarren") ironworks, in [Merthyr Tydfil](/wiki/Merthyr_Tydfil "Merthyr Tydfil"), to [Abercynon](/wiki/Abercynon "Abercynon") in South Wales.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) Accompanied by [Andrew Vivian](/wiki/Andrew_Vivian "Andrew Vivian"), it ran with mixed success.[[7]](#cite_note-ODNBTrevithick-7) The design incorporated a number of important innovations including the use of high-pressure steam which reduced the weight of the engine and increased its efficiency.

In 1812, [Matthew Murray](/wiki/Matthew_Murray "Matthew Murray")'s twin-cylinder [rack locomotive](/wiki/Rack_railway#Rolling_stock "Rack railway") *[Salamanca](/wiki/Salamanca_(locomotive) "Salamanca (locomotive)")* first ran on the [edge-railed](/wiki/Wagonway "Wagonway") [rack-and-pinion](/wiki/Rack_and_pinion "Rack and pinion") [Middleton Railway](/wiki/Middleton_Railway "Middleton Railway");[[8]](#cite_note-Young,1923-8) this is generally regarded as the first commercially successful locomotive.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10) Another well-known early locomotive was *[Puffing Billy](/wiki/Puffing_Billy_(locomotive) "Puffing Billy (locomotive)")*, built 1813–14 by engineer [William Hedley](/wiki/William_Hedley "William Hedley") for the Wylam Colliery near [Newcastle upon Tyne](/wiki/Newcastle_upon_Tyne "Newcastle upon Tyne"). This locomotive is the oldest preserved, and is on static display in the Science Museum, London. [George Stephenson](/wiki/George_Stephenson "George Stephenson") built *[Locomotion No. 1](/wiki/Locomotion_No._1 "Locomotion No. 1")* for the [Stockton & Darlington Railway](/wiki/Stockton_%26_Darlington_Railway "Stockton & Darlington Railway") in the north-east of England, which was the first public steam railway in the world. In 1829, his son Robert built *[The Rocket](/wiki/Stephenson%27s_Rocket "Stephenson's Rocket")* in Newcastle upon Tyne. Rocket was entered into, and won, the [Rainhill Trials](/wiki/Rainhill_Trials "Rainhill Trials"). This success led to the company emerging as the pre-eminent early builder of steam locomotives used on railways in the UK, US and much of Europe.[[11]](#cite_note-Ellis,1968-11) The [Liverpool & Manchester Railway](/wiki/Liverpool_%26_Manchester_Railway "Liverpool & Manchester Railway"), built by Stephenson, opened a year later making exclusive use of steam power for passenger and [goods trains](/wiki/Rail_freight_transport "Rail freight transport").

The steam locomotive remained by far the most common type of locomotive until after [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").[[12]](#cite_note-12) Steam locomotives are less efficient than modern diesel and electric locomotives, and a significantly larger workforce is required to operate and service them.[[13]](#cite_note-13) [British Rail](/wiki/British_Rail "British Rail") figures showed that the cost of crewing and fuelling a steam locomotive was about two and a half times larger than the cost of supporting an equivalent diesel locomotive, and the daily mileage they could run was lower.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Between about 1950 and 1970, the majority of steam locomotives were retired from commercial service and replaced with electric and diesel–electric locomotives.[[14]](#cite_note-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Diesel,1935-15) While North America transitioned from steam during the 1950s, and continental Europe by the 1970s, in other parts of the world, the transition happened later. Steam was a familiar technology that used widely-available fuels and in low-wage economies did not suffer as wide a cost disparity. It continued to be used in many countries until the end of the 20th century. By the end of the 20th century, almost the only steam power remaining in regular use around the world was on [heritage railways](/wiki/Heritage_railway "Heritage railway").

### Internal combustion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Locomotive&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Internal combustion")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Die_Daimler_Motor-Draisine_erstmals_im_Sommer_1887_zwischen_Esslingen_und_Kirchheim-Teck_erprobt.jpg/250px-Die_Daimler_Motor-Draisine_erstmals_im_Sommer_1887_zwischen_Esslingen_und_Kirchheim-Teck_erprobt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Die_Daimler_Motor-Draisine_erstmals_im_Sommer_1887_zwischen_Esslingen_und_Kirchheim-Teck_erprobt.jpg)

The 1887 Daimler draisine

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d0/Teplovoz_Eel2_%282%29.jpg/250px-Teplovoz_Eel2_%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Teplovoz_Eel2_(2).jpg)

The first useful diesel locomotive (a diesel–electric) for long distances, the [SŽD Eel2](/wiki/Russian_locomotive_class_E_el-2 "Russian locomotive class E el-2"), 1924 in [Kyiv](/wiki/Kyiv "Kyiv")

Main article: [Internal combustion locomotive](/wiki/Internal_combustion_locomotive "Internal combustion locomotive")

Internal combustion locomotives use an [internal combustion engine](/wiki/Internal_combustion_engine "Internal combustion engine"), connected to the [driving wheels](/wiki/Driving_wheel "Driving wheel") by a transmission. They typically keep the engine running at a near-constant speed whether the locomotive is stationary or moving. Internal combustion locomotives are categorised by their fuel type and sub-categorised by their transmission type.

The first internal combustion rail vehicle was a [kerosene](/wiki/Kerosene "Kerosene")-powered [draisine](/wiki/Draisine "Draisine") built by [Gottlieb Daimler](/wiki/Gottlieb_Daimler "Gottlieb Daimler") in 1887,[[16]](#cite_note-16) but this was not technically a locomotive as it carried a payload.

The earliest [gasoline](/wiki/Gasoline "Gasoline") locomotive in the western United States was built by the [Best Manufacturing Company](/wiki/Best_Manufacturing_Company "Best Manufacturing Company") in 1891 for [San Jose and Alum Rock Railroad](/wiki/Alum_Rock_Park#San_Jose_&_Alum_Rock_Park_Railway "Alum Rock Park"). It was only a limited success and was returned to Best in 1892.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The first commercially successful [petrol](/wiki/Petrol "Petrol") locomotive in the United Kingdom was a [petrol–mechanical locomotive](#Petrol–mechanical) built by the [Maudslay Motor Company](/wiki/Maudslay_Motor_Company "Maudslay Motor Company") in 1902, for the Deptford Cattle Market in [London](/wiki/London "London"). It was an 80 hp locomotive using a three-cylinder vertical petrol engine, with a two speed mechanical gearbox.

In 1903, the Hungarian [Weitzer railmotor](/wiki/Weitzer_railmotor "Weitzer railmotor") was the world's first [petrol electric](/wiki/Petrol-electric "Petrol-electric") locomotive.

#### Diesel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Locomotive&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Diesel")]

Main article: [Diesel locomotive](/wiki/Diesel_locomotive "Diesel locomotive")

Diesel locomotives are powered by [diesel engines](/wiki/Diesel_engine "Diesel engine"). In the early days of diesel propulsion development, various transmission systems were employed with varying degrees of success, with electric transmission proving to be the most popular. In 1914, [Hermann Lemp](/wiki/Hermann_Lemp "Hermann Lemp"), a [General Electric](/wiki/General_Electric "General Electric") electrical engineer, developed and patented a reliable [direct current](/wiki/Direct_current "Direct current") electrical control system (subsequent improvements were also patented by Lemp).[[18]](#cite_note-18) Lemp's design used a single lever to control both engine and generator in a coordinated fashion, and was the [prototype](/wiki/Prototype "Prototype") for all [diesel–electric locomotive](/wiki/Diesel_locomotive#Diesel–electric "Diesel locomotive") control. In 1917–18, GE produced three experimental diesel–electric locomotives using Lemp's control design.[[19]](#cite_note-SDSG-19) In 1924, a diesel–electric locomotive ([Eel2](/wiki/Russian_locomotive_class_E_el-2 "Russian locomotive class E el-2") original number Юэ 001/Yu-e 001) started operations. It had been designed by a team led by [Yury Lomonosov](/wiki/Yury_Lomonosov "Yury Lomonosov") and built 1923–1924 by [Maschinenfabrik Esslingen](/wiki/Maschinenfabrik_Esslingen "Maschinenfabrik Esslingen") in Germany. It had five driving axles (1'E1'). After several test rides, it hauled trains for almost three decades from 1925 to 1954.[[20]](#cite_note-20)

### Electric

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Locomotive&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Electric")]

Main article: [Electric locomotive](/wiki/Electric_locomotive "Electric locomotive")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Lokomotif_listrik_ESS_3200_di_Stasiun_Tanjung_Priuk..jpg/250px-Lokomotif_listrik_ESS_3200_di_Stasiun_Tanjung_Priuk..jpg)](/wiki/File:Lokomotif_listrik_ESS_3200_di_Stasiun_Tanjung_Priuk..jpg)

The [ESS 3200](/wiki/ESS_3200_class "ESS 3200 class") electric locomotive was introduced in 1925 in the [Dutch East Indies](/wiki/Dutch_East_Indies "Dutch East Indies"), now [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia "Indonesia").

An electric locomotive is a locomotive powered only by electricity. Electricity is supplied to moving trains with a (nearly) continuous [conductor](/wiki/Electrical_conductor "Electrical conductor") running along the track that usually takes one of three forms: an [overhead line](/wiki/Overhead_line "Overhead line"), suspended from poles or towers along the track or from structure or tunnel ceilings; a [third rail](/wiki/Third_rail "Third rail") mounted at track level; or an onboard [battery](/wiki/Electric_battery "Electric battery"). Both overhead wire and third-rail systems usually use the running rails as the return conductor but some systems use a separate fourth rail for this purpose. The type of electrical power used is either [direct current](/wiki/Direct_current "Direct current") (DC) or [alternating current](/wiki/Alternating_current "Alternating current") (AC).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e4/Eastleigh_Works_geograph-2383942-by-Ben-Brooksbank.jpg/250px-Eastleigh_Works_geograph-2383942-by-Ben-Brooksbank.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eastleigh_Works_geograph-2383942-by-Ben-Brooksbank.jpg)

[Southern Railway (UK) 20002](/wiki/British_Rail_Class_70_(electric) "British Rail Class 70 (electric)") was equipped with both a pantograph and contact shoes.

Various collection methods exist: a [trolley pole](/wiki/Trolley_pole "Trolley pole"), which is a long flexible pole that engages the line with a wheel or shoe; a [bow collector](/wiki/Bow_collector "Bow collector"), which is a frame that holds a long collecting rod against the wire; a [pantograph](/wiki/Pantograph_(transport) "Pantograph (transport)"), which is a hinged frame that holds the collecting shoes against the wire in a fixed geometry; or a [contact shoe](/wiki/Current_collector#Contact_shoe "Current collector"), which is a shoe in contact with the third rail. Of the three, the pantograph method is best suited for high-speed operation.

Electric locomotives almost universally use axle-hung traction motors, with one motor for each powered axle. In this arrangement, one side of the motor housing is supported by plain bearings riding on a ground and polished journal that is integral to the axle. The other side of the housing has a tongue-shaped protuberance that engages a matching slot in the truck (bogie) bolster, its purpose being to act as a torque reaction device, as well as a support. Power transfer from motor to axle is effected by [spur gearing](/wiki/Gear#Spur "Gear"), in which a [pinion](/wiki/Pinion "Pinion") on the motor shaft engages a [bull gear](/wiki/List_of_gear_nomenclature#Bull_gear "List of gear nomenclature") on the axle. Both gears are enclosed in a liquid-tight housing containing lubricating oil. The type of service in which the locomotive is used dictates the gear ratio employed. Numerically high ratios are commonly found on freight units, whereas numerically low ratios are typical of passenger engines.

Electricity is typically generated in large and relatively efficient [generating stations](/wiki/Power_station "Power station"), transmitted to the railway network and distributed to the trains. Some electric railways have their own dedicated generating stations and [transmission lines](/wiki/Electric_power_transmission "Electric power transmission") but most purchase power from an [electric utility](/wiki/Electric_utility "Electric utility"). The railway usually provides its own distribution lines, switches and [transformers](/wiki/Transformer "Transformer").

Electric locomotives usually cost 20% less than diesel locomotives, their maintenance costs are 25–35% lower, and cost up to 50% less to run.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

#### Direct current

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Locomotive&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Direct current")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/24/First_electric_locomotive%2C_built_in_1879_by_Werner_von_Siemens.jpg/250px-First_electric_locomotive%2C_built_in_1879_by_Werner_von_Siemens.jpg)](/wiki/File:First_electric_locomotive,_built_in_1879_by_Werner_von_Siemens.jpg)

Werner von Siemens experimental DC electric train, 1879

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/B-and-O_electric.jpg/250px-B-and-O_electric.jpg)](/wiki/File:B-and-O_electric.jpg)

Baltimore & Ohio electric engine, 1895

The earliest systems were [DC](/wiki/Direct_current "Direct current") systems. The first electric passenger train was presented by [Werner von Siemens](/wiki/Werner_von_Siemens "Werner von Siemens") at [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin") in 1879. The locomotive was driven by a 2.2 kW, series-wound motor, and the train, consisting of the locomotive and three cars, reached a speed of 13 km/h. During four months, the train carried 90,000 passengers on a 300-meter-long (980-foot) circular track. The electricity (150 V DC) was supplied through a third insulated rail between the tracks. A contact roller was used to collect the electricity. The world's first electric tram line opened in Lichterfelde near Berlin, Germany, in 1881. It was built by Werner von Siemens (see [Gross-Lichterfelde Tramway](/wiki/Gross-Lichterfelde_Tramway "Gross-Lichterfelde Tramway") and [Berlin Straßenbahn](/wiki/Trams_in_Berlin "Trams in Berlin")). The [Volk's Electric Railway](/wiki/Volk%27s_Electric_Railway "Volk's Electric Railway") opened in 1883 in Brighton, and is the oldest surviving electric railway. Also in 1883, [Mödling and Hinterbrühl Tram](/wiki/M%C3%B6dling_and_Hinterbr%C3%BChl_Tram "Mödling and Hinterbrühl Tram") opened near Vienna in Austria. It was the first in the world in regular service powered from an overhead line. Five years later, in the U.S. electric [trolleys](/wiki/Tram "Tram") were pioneered in 1888 on the [Richmond Union Passenger Railway](/wiki/Richmond_Union_Passenger_Railway "Richmond Union Passenger Railway"), using equipment designed by [Frank J. Sprague](/wiki/Frank_J._Sprague "Frank J. Sprague").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

The first electrically worked [underground](/wiki/Rapid_transit "Rapid transit") line was the [City & South London Railway](/wiki/City_%26_South_London_Railway "City & South London Railway"), prompted by a clause in its enabling act prohibiting use of steam power.[[23]](#cite_note-23) It opened in 1890, using electric locomotives built by [Mather & Platt](/wiki/Mather_%26_Platt "Mather & Platt"). Electricity quickly became the power supply of choice for subways, abetted by the Sprague's invention of [multiple-unit train control](/wiki/Multiple-unit_train_control "Multiple-unit train control") in 1897.

The first use of electrification on a main line was on a four-mile stretch of the [Baltimore Belt Line](/wiki/Baltimore_Belt_Line "Baltimore Belt Line") of the [Baltimore & Ohio](/wiki/Baltimore_%26_Ohio "Baltimore & Ohio") (B&O) in 1895 connecting the main portion of the B&O to the new line to New York through a series of tunnels around the edges of Baltimore's downtown. Three [Bo+Bo](/wiki/UIC_classification_of_locomotive_axle_arrangements "UIC classification of locomotive axle arrangements") units were initially used, at the south end of the electrified section; they coupled onto the locomotive and train and pulled it through the tunnels.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

DC was used on earlier systems. These systems were gradually replaced by AC. Today, almost all main-line railways use AC systems. DC systems are confined mostly to urban transit such as metro systems, light rail and trams, where power requirement is less.

#### Alternating current

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Locomotive&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Alternating current")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Ganz_engine_Valtellina.jpg/250px-Ganz_engine_Valtellina.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ganz_engine_Valtellina.jpg)

A prototype of a Ganz AC electric locomotive in Valtellina, Italy, 1901

The first practical [AC](/wiki/Alternating_current "Alternating current") electric locomotive was designed by [Charles Brown](/wiki/Charles_Eugene_Lancelot_Brown "Charles Eugene Lancelot Brown"), then working for [Oerlikon](/wiki/Maschinenfabrik_Oerlikon "Maschinenfabrik Oerlikon"), Zürich. In 1891, Brown had demonstrated long-distance power transmission, using [three-phase AC](/wiki/Three-phase_electric_power "Three-phase electric power"), between a [hydro-electric plant](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity") at [Lauffen am Neckar](/wiki/Lauffen_am_Neckar "Lauffen am Neckar") and [Frankfurt](/wiki/Frankfurt "Frankfurt") am Main West, a distance of 280 km. Using experience he had gained while working for [Jean Heilmann](/wiki/Heilmann_locomotive "Heilmann locomotive") on steam–electric locomotive designs, Brown observed that [three-phase motors](/wiki/AC_motor#Three-phase_AC_synchronous_motors "AC motor") had a higher power-to-weight ratio than [DC](/wiki/Direct_current "Direct current") motors and, because of the absence of a [commutator](/wiki/Commutator_(electric) "Commutator (electric)"), were simpler to manufacture and maintain.[[a]](#cite_note-26) However, they were much larger than the DC motors of the time and could not be mounted in underfloor [bogies](/wiki/Bogie "Bogie"): they could only be carried within locomotive bodies.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuffy2003129-27)

In 1894, Hungarian engineer [Kálmán Kandó](/wiki/K%C3%A1lm%C3%A1n_Kand%C3%B3 "Kálmán Kandó") developed a new type 3-phase asynchronous electric drive motors and generators for electric locomotives. The new 3-phase asynchronous electric drive motors were more effective than the synchronous electric motors of earlier locomotive designs.
Kandó's early 1894 designs were first applied in a short three-phase AC tramway in Evian-les-Bains (France), which was constructed between 1896 and 1898.[[27]](#cite_note-28)[[28]](#cite_note-29)[[29]](#cite_note-30)[[30]](#cite_note-31)[[31]](#cite_note-32) In 1918,[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuffy2003137-33) Kandó invented and developed the [rotary phase converter](/wiki/Rotary_phase_converter "Rotary phase converter"), enabling electric locomotives to use three-phase motors whilst supplied via a single overhead wire, carrying the simple industrial frequency (50 Hz) single phase AC of the high voltage national networks.[[33]](#cite_note-Patent_Office-34)

In 1896, Oerlikon installed the first commercial example of the system on the [Lugano Tramway](/wiki/Trams_in_Lugano "Trams in Lugano"). Each 30-tonne locomotive had two 110 kW (150 hp) motors run by three-phase 750 V 40 Hz fed from double overhead lines. Three-phase motors run at constant speed and provide [regenerative braking](/wiki/Regenerative_brake "Regenerative brake"), and are well suited to steeply graded routes, and the first main-line three-phase locomotives were supplied by Brown (by then in partnership with [Walter Boveri](/wiki/Brown,_Boveri_%26_Cie "Brown, Boveri & Cie")) in 1899 on the 40 km [Burgdorf—Thun line](/wiki/List_of_railway_electrification_systems#Burgdorf-Thun_Bahn "List of railway electrification systems"), Switzerland. The first implementation of industrial frequency single-phase AC supply for locomotives came from Oerlikon in 1901, using the designs of [Hans Behn-Eschenburg](/wiki/Hans_Behn-Eschenburg "Hans Behn-Eschenburg") and [Emil Huber-Stockar](/wiki/Emil_Huber-Stockar "Emil Huber-Stockar"); installation on the Seebach-Wettingen line of the Swiss Federal Railways was completed in 1904. The 15 kV, 50 Hz 345 kW (460 hp), 48 tonne locomotives used transformers and rotary converters to power DC traction motors.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuffy2003124-35)

Italian railways were the first in the world to introduce electric traction for the entire length of a main line rather than just a short stretch. The 106 km Valtellina line was opened on 4 September 1902, designed by Kandó and a team from the Ganz works.[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDuffy2003120–121-36)[[33]](#cite_note-Patent_Office-34) The electrical system was three-phase at 3 kV 15 Hz. The voltage was significantly higher than used earlier and it required new designs for electric motors and switching devices.[[36]](#cite_note-Kalman_Kando2-37)[[37]](#cite_note-38) The three-phase two-wire system was used on several railways in Northern Italy and became known as "the Italian system". Kandó was invited in 1905 to undertake the management of Società Italiana Westinghouse and led the development of several Italian electric locomotives.[[36]](#cite_note-Kalman_Kando2-37)

#### Battery–electric

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Locomotive&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Battery–electric")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Battery_loco_16_at_West_Ham.JPG/250px-Battery_loco_16_at_West_Ham.JPG)](/wiki/File:Battery_loco_16_at_West_Ham.JPG)

A [London Underground](/wiki/London_Underground "London Underground") battery–electric locomotive used for hauling engineers' trains, at [West Ham station](/wiki/West_Ham_station "West Ham station")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Wingrove_%26_Rogers_6092.jpg/250px-Wingrove_%26_Rogers_6092.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wingrove_%26_Rogers_6092.jpg)

A narrow-gauge battery–electric locomotive used for mining

A battery–electric locomotive (or battery locomotive) is an electric locomotive powered by onboard [batteries](/wiki/Electric_battery "Electric battery"); a kind of [battery electric vehicle](/wiki/Battery_electric_vehicle "Battery electric vehicle").

Such locomotives are used where a conventional diesel or electric locomotive would be unsuitable. An example is maintenance trains on electrified lines when the electricity supply is turned off. Another use is in industrial facilities where a combustion-powered locomotive (i.e., [steam-](/wiki/Steam_locomotive "Steam locomotive") or [diesel-powered](/wiki/Diesel_locomotive "Diesel locomotive")) could cause a safety issue due to the risks of fire, explosion or fumes in a confined space. Battery locomotives are preferred for mines where gas could be ignited by [trolley-powered](/wiki/Trolley_pole "Trolley pole") units [arcing](/wiki/Electric_arc "Electric arc") at the collection shoes, or where [electrical resistance](/wiki/Electrical_resistance_and_conductance "Electrical resistance and conductance") could develop in the supply or return circuits, especially at rail joints, and allow dangerous current leakage into the ground.[[38]](#cite_note-39) Battery locomotives in over-the-road service can recharge while absorbing dynamic-braking energy.[[39]](#cite_note-40)

The first known electric locomotive was built in 1837 by chemist [Robert Davidson](/wiki/Robert_Davidson_(inventor) "Robert Davidson (inventor)") of [Aberdeen](/wiki/Aberdeen "Aberdeen"), and it was powered by [galvanic cells](/wiki/Galvanic_cell "Galvanic cell") (batteries). Davidson later built a larger locomotive named *Galvani*, exhibited at the [Royal Scottish Society of Arts](/wiki/Royal_Scottish_Society_of_Arts "Royal Scottish Society of Arts") Exhibition in 1841. The 7 long tons (7.1 t; 7.8 short tons) vehicle had two [direct-drive](/wiki/Direct-drive_mechanism "Direct-drive mechanism") [reluctance motors](/wiki/Reluctance_motor "Reluctance motor"), with fixed electromagnets acting on iron bars attached to a wooden cylinder on each axle, and simple [commutators](/wiki/Commutator_(electric) "Commutator (electric)"). It hauled a load of 6 long tons (6.1 t; 6.7 short tons) at 4 mph (6.4 km/h) for a distance of one and a half miles (2.4 kilometres). It was tested on the [Edinburgh and Glasgow Railway](/wiki/Edinburgh_and_Glasgow_Railway "Edinburgh and Glasgow Railway") in September of the following year, but the limited power from batteries prevented its general use.[[40]](#cite_note-41)[[41]](#cite_note-42)[[42]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-43)

Another example was the [Kennecott Copper Mine](/wiki/Kennecott,_Alaska "Kennecott, Alaska"), [Latouche, Alaska](/w/index.php?title=Latouche,_Alaska&action=edit&redlink=1 "Latouche, Alaska (page does not exist)"), where in 1917 the underground haulage ways were widened to enable working by two 4.5 short tons (4.1 t; 4.0 long tons) battery locomotives.[[43]](#cite_note-44) In 1928, Kennecott Copper ordered four 700-series electric locomotives with on-board batteries. These locomotives weighed 85 short tons (77 t; 76 long tons) and operated on 750-volt overhead trolley wire with considerable further range whilst running on batteries.[[44]](#cite_note-45) The locomotives provided several decades of service using [Nickel–iron battery](/wiki/Nickel%E2%80%93iron_battery "Nickel–iron battery") (Edison) technology. These were later replaced with [lead-acid batteries](/wiki/Lead%E2%80%93acid_battery "Lead–acid battery"), and the locomotives were retired shortly afterward. All four locomotives were donated to museums, but one was scrapped. The others can be seen at the [Boone and Scenic Valley Railroad](/wiki/Boone_and_Scenic_Valley_Railroad "Boone and Scenic Valley Railroad"), Iowa, and at the [Western Railway Museum](/wiki/Western_Railway_Museum "Western Railway Museum") in Rio Vista, California. The [Toronto Transit Commission](/wiki/Toronto_Transit_Commission "Toronto Transit Commission") previously operated a battery electric locomotive built by [Nippon Sharyo](/wiki/Nippon_Sharyo "Nippon Sharyo") in 1968 and retired in 2009.[[45]](#cite_note-46)

London Underground regularly operates [battery–electric locomotives](/wiki/London_Underground_battery%E2%80%93electric_locomotives "London Underground battery–electric locomotives") for general maintenance work.

#### Fireless

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Locomotive&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Fireless")]

Main article: [Fireless locomotive](/wiki/Fireless_locomotive "Fireless locomotive")

#### Atomic–electric

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Locomotive&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Atomic–electric")]

In the early 1950s, Lyle Borst of the [University of Utah](/wiki/University_of_Utah "University of Utah") was given funding by various US railroad line and manufacturers to study the feasibility of an electric-drive locomotive, in which an onboard atomic reactor produced the steam to generate the electricity. At that time, atomic power was not fully understood; Borst believed the biggest issue was the price of uranium. With his atomic locomotive, the center section would have a 200 short tons (181 t; 179 long tons) reactor chamber and steel walls 5 ft (1.5 m) thick to prevent radiation leaks in case of accidents. He estimated a cost to manufacture atomic locomotives with 7,000 hp (5.2 MW) engines at approximately $1,200,000 each.[[46]](#cite_note-47) Consequently, trains with onboard nuclear generators were generally deemed unfeasible due to prohibitive costs.

#### Fuel cell–electric

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Locomotive&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Fuel cell–electric")]

Main article: [Hydrail](/wiki/Hydrail "Hydrail")

In 2002, the first 3.6 t (4.0 short tons; 3.5 long tons), 17 kW (23 hp) [hydrogen](/wiki/Hydrogen "Hydrogen")-(fuel-cell)–powered mining locomotive was demonstrated in [Val-d'Or](/wiki/Val-d%27Or "Val-d'Or"), [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"). In 2007 the educational mini-hydrail in [Kaohsiung](/wiki/Kaohsiung "Kaohsiung"), [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan") went into service. The [Railpower GG20B](/wiki/Railpower_GG20B "Railpower GG20B") is another example of a fuel cell–electric locomotive.

#### Hybrid locomotives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Locomotive&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Hybrid locomotives")]

Main article: [Hybrid train](/wiki/Hybrid_train "Hybrid train")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Bombardier_ALP-45DP_at_Innotrans_2010.jpg/250px-Bombardier_ALP-45DP_at_Innotrans_2010.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bombardier_ALP-45DP_at_Innotrans_2010.jpg)

[Bombardier ALP-45DP](/wiki/Bombardier_ALP-45DP "Bombardier ALP-45DP"), at the Innotrans convention in [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin")

There are many different types of hybrid or dual-mode locomotives using two or more types of motive power. The most common hybrids are [electro-diesel locomotives](/wiki/Electro-diesel_locomotive "Electro-diesel locomotive") powered either from an electricity supply or else by an onboard [diesel engine](/wiki/Diesel_engine "Diesel engine"). These are used to provide continuous journeys along routes that are only partly electrified. Examples include the [EMD FL9](/wiki/EMD_FL9 "EMD FL9") and [Bombardier ALP-45DP](/wiki/Bombardier_ALP-45DP "Bombardier ALP-45DP")

## Use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Locomotive&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Use")]

There are three main uses of locomotives in [rail transport operations](/wiki/Rail_transport_operations "Rail transport operations"): hauling [passenger](/wiki/Passenger "Passenger") trains, [freight](/wiki/Freight "Freight") trains, and [switching](/wiki/Shunting_(rail) "Shunting (rail)") (UK English: shunting).

Freight locomotives are normally designed to deliver high starting [tractive effort](/wiki/Tractive_effort "Tractive effort") and high sustained power. This allows them to start and move long, heavy trains, but usually comes at the cost of relatively low maximum speeds. Passenger locomotives usually develop lower starting tractive effort but are able to operate at the high speeds required to maintain passenger schedules. Mixed-traffic locomotives (US English: general purpose or road switcher locomotives) meant for both passenger and freight trains do not develop as much starting tractive effort as a freight locomotive but are able to haul heavier trains than a passenger locomotive.[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Locomotive#Dubious "Talk:Locomotive")*]

Most steam locomotives have reciprocating engines, with pistons coupled to the driving wheels by means of connecting rods, with no intervening gearbox. This means the combination of starting tractive effort and maximum speed is greatly influenced by the diameter of the driving wheels. Steam locomotives intended for freight service generally have smaller diameter driving wheels than passenger locomotives.

In diesel-electric and electric locomotives the control system between the [traction motors](/wiki/Traction_motor "Traction motor") and [axles](/wiki/Axle "Axle") adapts the power output to the rails for freight or passenger service. Passenger locomotives may include other features, such as [head-end power](/wiki/Head-end_power "Head-end power") (also referred to as hotel power or electric train supply) or a [steam generator](/wiki/Steam_generator_(railroad) "Steam generator (railroad)").

Some locomotives are designed specifically to work [steep grade railways](/wiki/Steep_grade_railway "Steep grade railway"), and feature extensive additional braking mechanisms and sometimes rack and pinion. Steam locomotives built for steep [rack and pinion railways](/wiki/Rack_railway "Rack railway") frequently have the boiler tilted relative to the [locomotive frame](/wiki/Locomotive_frame "Locomotive frame"), so that the boiler remains roughly level on steep grades.

Locomotives are also used on some high-speed trains. Some of them are operated in [push–pull formation](/wiki/Push%E2%80%93pull_train "Push–pull train") with trailer [control cars](/wiki/Control_car "Control car") at another end of a train, which often have a cabin with the same design as a cabin of locomotive; examples of such trains with conventional locomotives are [Railjet](/wiki/Railjet "Railjet") and [Intercity 225](/wiki/Intercity_225 "Intercity 225").

Also many high-speed trains, including all [TGV](/wiki/TGV "TGV"), many [Talgo](/wiki/Talgo "Talgo") (250 / 350 / Avril / XXI), some [Korea Train Express](/wiki/Korea_Train_Express "Korea Train Express"), [ICE 1](/wiki/ICE_1 "ICE 1")/[ICE 2](/wiki/ICE_2 "ICE 2") and [Intercity 125](/wiki/Intercity_125 "Intercity 125"), use dedicated [power cars](/wiki/Power_car "Power car"), which do not have places for passengers and technically are special single-ended locomotives. The difference from conventional locomotives is that these power cars are integral part of a train and are not adapted for operation with any other types of passenger coaches. On the other hand, many high-speed trains such as the [Shinkansen](/wiki/Shinkansen "Shinkansen") network never use locomotives. Instead of locomotive-like power-cars, they use [electric multiple units](/wiki/Electric_multiple_unit "Electric multiple unit") (EMUs) or diesel multiple units (DMUs) – passenger cars that also have traction motors and power equipment. Using dedicated locomotive-like power cars allows for a high ride quality and less electrical equipment;[[47]](#cite_note-hata-48)
but EMUs have less axle weight, which reduces maintenance costs, and EMUs also have higher acceleration and higher seating capacity.[[47]](#cite_note-hata-48)
Also some trains, including [TGV PSE](/wiki/TGV_PSE "TGV PSE"), [TGV TMST](/wiki/TGV_TMST "TGV TMST") and [TGV V150](/wiki/Project_V150 "Project V150"), use both non-passenger power cars and additional passenger motor cars.

## Operational role

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Locomotive&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Operational role")]

Locomotives occasionally work in a specific role, such as:

* **Train engine** is the technical name for a locomotive attached to the front of a railway [train](/wiki/Train "Train") to haul that train. Alternatively, where facilities exist for [push–pull](/wiki/Push%E2%80%93pull_train "Push–pull train") operation, the train engine might be attached to the rear of the train;
* [Pilot engine](/wiki/Pilot_engine "Pilot engine") – a locomotive attached in front of the train engine, to enable [double-heading](/wiki/Double-heading "Double-heading");
* [Banking engine](/wiki/Banking_engine "Banking engine") – a locomotive temporarily assisting a train from the rear, due to a difficult start or a sharp incline gradient;
* [Light engine](/wiki/Light_engine "Light engine") – a locomotive operating without a train behind it, for relocation or operational reasons. Occasionally, a light engine is referred to as a train in and of itself.
* [Station pilot](/wiki/Station_pilot "Station pilot") – a locomotive used to [shunt](/wiki/Shunting_(rail) "Shunting (rail)") passenger trains at a [railway station](/wiki/Railway_station "Railway station").

## Wheel arrangement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Locomotive&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Wheel arrangement")]

Main article: [Wheel arrangement](/wiki/Wheel_arrangement "Wheel arrangement")

The wheel arrangement of a locomotive describes how many wheels it has; common methods include the [AAR wheel arrangement](/wiki/AAR_wheel_arrangement "AAR wheel arrangement"), [UIC classification](/wiki/UIC_classification "UIC classification"), and [Whyte notation](/wiki/Whyte_notation "Whyte notation") systems.

## Remote control locomotives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Locomotive&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Remote control locomotives")]

Main article: [Remote control locomotive](/wiki/Remote_control_locomotive "Remote control locomotive")

In the second half of the twentieth century [remote control locomotives](/wiki/Remote_control_locomotive "Remote control locomotive") started to enter service in switching operations, being remotely controlled by an operator outside of the locomotive cab.
The main benefit is one operator can control the loading of grain, coal, gravel, etc. into the cars. In addition, the same operator can move the train as needed. Thus, the locomotive is loaded or unloaded in about a third of the time.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
